external.description:
News-Websites verlieren gegen Social Media und AI: So verändert sich der Nachrichtenkonsum 2026 drastisch.

Nachrichtenwandel 🌍 Immer mehr Menschen verlassen klassische #News Websites und informieren sich stattdessen über #sozialeNetzwerke, #Video #Plattformen und #AI Tools.

Social Media dominiert 📱 In vielen Ländern sind Instagram, Facebook, TikTok und YouTube inzwischen wichtiger für Nachrichten als die Angebote der Verlage selbst.

KI als Nachrichtenquelle 🤖 Chatbots wie ChatGPT oder Gemini werden zunehmend genutzt, um Nachrichten zusammenzufassen, Hintergründe zu erklären und Fragen direkt zu beantworten.

YouTube gewinnt 🎥 Besonders bei jungen Nutzern wächst die Bedeutung von YouTube, während Nachrichtenvideos auf den eigenen Webseiten der Medienhäuser deutlich an Reichweite verlieren.

Verlage kämpfen 📰 Sinkende Besucherzahlen, weniger direkte Zugriffe und schwache Zahlungsbereitschaft erhöhen den Druck auf traditionelle Medienangebote.

Objektivität zählt ⚖️ Trotz wachsender Polarisierung bevorzugt die Mehrheit weiterhin Nachrichtenquellen, die als neutral und ausgewogen wahrgenommen werden.

👉 eicker.TV ▹ / Tech / Media / Politik / Business ⏻ eicker.BEratung von Gerrit Eicker aus Münster

The Warrior-Witches of Ukraine’s Resistance
An underground intelligence network uses subterfuge and honey traps to direct drone strikes deep inside Russian-occupied territory.

By Ken Harbaugh
An illustration of a rocket headed from a shadowy female figure toward a soldier whose face is illuminated by the glow of his phone
Illustration by Ann Kiernan
June 21, 2026

Share

Save

For several months last year, a Ukrainian housewife, 35 and lonely in a marriage that had gone cold, traded WhatsApp messages with a Chechen commander, Achmad, stationed somewhere in Ukraine’s occupied south. They wrote about their days, their disappointments, what they hoped to do when the war ended. She asked about the front. He told her.

“Send me a picture,” she said. “I want to see your life.”

One afternoon, he obliged—a photograph taken inside the barracks, of himself and another soldier grinning for the camera. Behind them, pinned to the wall, was a map of the compound showing the unit’s position.

The housewife did not exist. “She” was a middle-aged officer named Serhiy working for Ukraine’s military-intelligence directorate, part of a concerted effort to draw secrets from the men sent to occupy his country.

“Serhiy was great at flirting,” his commander told me. “Guys in our team started asking him for dating advice.” Shortly after Achmad sent that photograph, the coordinates it revealed were struck by a Ukrainian drone.

She wears a  sheer, pleated linen kalasiris that clings to her curves, he fabric dyed a deep indigo with intricate gold-thread hieroglyphs running along the hem. Her dark eyes, accentuated with black eyeliner gaze across the river with the knowing intensity of a priestess, while a golden collar of lapis lazuli and carnelian beads rests against her collarbones, catching the light like scattered embers. The Nile shimmers behind her, alive with lotus blossoms and the distant silhouettes of papyrus boats, while the ghostly reflection of one pyramid shimmers in the water—half mirage, half prophecy. A single white ibis perches on a nearby stone, as if awaiting her command, and the wind lifts strands of her braided, henna-tipped hair like tendrils of smoke from an unseen altar. record.text: I'm happy with the way I am... what do they want from me?

“And in ten years? Captain.”

The older me said it as if it were an undeniable fact.

For a moment, I imagined it.

The rank. The command. The prestige.

Then I looked away.

“Maybe.”

But somehow, that still doesn’t sound like the future I want.
